[
  {
    "id":"agent_1_001",
    "source": "U",
    "topic": "Ignition source",
    "claim": "The ignition that triggered the fire and subsequent explosion was caused by mechanical sparks generated by the armored face conveyor (AFC) chain operating in a methane‑rich sub‑conveyor zone.",
    "evidence": "Physical inspection found an angle grinder with 6 used disks (U-A1) and an aerosol can marked flammable on the shearer (U-A1); CO sensor data showed a sharp rise from 0.0004% to 0.0009% indicating combustion of solid material rather than pure methane (U-A1); witness testimony reported sparking from the conveyor chain during operation (K-A4); physical tests confirmed conveyor sparking is possible and the sparking zone coincided with elevated methane concentrations in sections 128‑142 (K-A4); analysis of ignition categories per EN 1127-1 indicates mechanical sparks are the most probable ignition source when electrical, self‑ignition, and chemical sources are excluded (D-A5).",
    "warrant": "The presence of ignition‑capable items and CO evidence of solid material combustion, combined with documented conveyor sparking in a zone with high methane, shows that mechanical sparks from the AFC chain could have ignited the methane‑air mixture.",
    "confidence": 0.78,
    "cause_categories": ["TC-02"]
  },
  {
    "id": "agent_2_001",
    "source": "K",
    "topic": "Methane source",
    "claim": "Elevated methane in the goaf and upper longwall originated primarily from continuous release of methane from the K2 companion seam due to abutment‑pressure decompression, which was not mitigated by degasification.",
    "evidence": "Post‑accident methane survey showed concentrations up to 17.9% in sections 128‑142 with stagnant airflow (K-A2); K2 seam thickness ~5 m and CH4 content ~15 m3/t, with no pre‑drainage boreholes (D-A1); geological conditions (3.35 m separation, weak rock) create decompression zones that release methane into the sub‑conveyor space (K-A2).",
    "warrant": "The K2 seam lies 3.35 m below the working face, producing a pressure‑release zone where fracturing liberates methane into a low‑velocity sub‑conveyor area where ventilation cannot dilute it, leading to explosive concentrations.",
    "confidence": 0.85,
    "cause_categories": ["TC-01", "TC-07"]
  },
  {
    "id": "agent_3_001",
    "source": "D",
    "topic": "Explosion type",
    "claim": "The accident began with a methane deflagration that displaced methane into the workings, which then ignited a secondary coal‑dust explosion that propagated throughout the mine.",
    "evidence": "Miner’s gas detector recorded 99.99% methane and temperature rise from 21 °C to 45 °C, pressure increase 103→109 (K-A7); fire marks and CO increase at 02:24 indicate methane combustion (D-A6); video showed workers breathing without respirators after shock wave, indicating lack of coal dust (D-A8); explosion propagated 4+ km along conveyor routes with coal‑dust accumulations observed on equipment (D-A9).",
    "warrant": "The high methane concentration and temperature rise demonstrate a methane‑air deflagration; the subsequent long‑range propagation following conveyor routes and the presence of coal‑dust deposits are characteristic of a dust explosion, not a pure methane explosion.",
    "confidence": 0.75,
    "cause_categories": ["TC-01", "TC-10"]
  },
  {
    "id": "agent_4_001",
    "source": "D",
    "topic": "Ventilation",
    "claim": "Although overall ventilation met design flow rates, the combined 1K‑N‑N‑vt ventilation scheme created a stagnant sub‑conveyor zone where methane accumulated to explosive levels despite acceptable average concentrations.",
    "evidence": "Airflow measured 2572 m3/min vs design 2705 m3/min, leakage coefficient 1.3, and no borehole drainage of K2 (D-A3); CH4 in outgoing stream stayed below LEL, but sub‑conveyor zone remained stagnant (D-A3); CO sensor rise at 02:24 shows localized combustion (D-A6).",
    "warrant": "The ventilation quantity was adequate for design, yet the geometry of the 1K‑N‑N‑vt scheme produced a low‑velocity area beneath the conveyor where methane released from K2 could accumulate without dilution, creating an explosive atmosphere not captured by routine monitoring.",
    "confidence": 0.80,
    "cause_categories": ["TC-06"]
  },
  {
    "id": "agent_5_001",
    "source": "K",
    "topic": "Explosion location",
    "claim": "The primary explosion originated in the goaf near section 20 of longwall 48K3‑Z, about 120 m from the face, and was a methane deflagration that subsequently triggered the coal‑dust explosion.",
    "evidence": "Scorched support coatings on sections 20‑8 displaced toward the coal face, indicating pressure wave from goaf direction (D-A8); seismic events recorded at 02:43:32 and 02:4
